tldr: Headlines across all 12 outlet groups frame Ismael 'El Mayo' Zambada's life sentence as a clear criminal-justice endpoint; Mexico's outlets diverge sharply by adding layers of ongoing cartel violence, US political pressure on Mexican institutions, and implicit questions about El Mayo's status as a cooperative witness — angles absent from every other national headline layer.

frames.sub_frame: US justice targets Mexico's political class

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline to frame the sentence through the implicit suggestion of a protected-witness arrangement ('aroma de testigo protegido'), raising a legal-process question absent from every other outlet's headline.
Only headline that foregrounds the $15B financial forfeiture as a co-equal element of justice, pairing punishment with the scale of profit recovery in a way no other outlet's title does.

Only outlet to headline a concurrent Sinaloa Cartel massacre ('masacre en Zacatecas'), anchoring the sentencing moment inside an ongoing cycle of cartel violence rather than presenting it as legal closure.

bottom_line: Internationally, the headline layer treats El Mayo's life sentence as a definitive crime-and-punishment event; Mexico's domestic outlets uniquely frame it as an unresolved security and political crisis — highlighting cartel violence, US institutional pressure on Mexican politics, and unresolved questions about judicial cooperation.
